title: The Classification Head
concept: After the convolutional feature extractor, a CNN uses a classification head — often flattening or global pooling followed by dense layers — to convert final feature maps into class logits.

exercises.prompt: Recap: If a CNN's last feature block outputs an 8x8x32 feature stack, what job still remains?

options.text: Turn those learned features into class scores with a classification head
Convert the feature stack back into untouched raw pixels before predicting
Add more RGB channels until it matches the input image again

exercises.explain: Exactly — feature maps are useful, but the model still needs a final step that turns them into a class decision.

cards.title: Someone has to make the final call
cards.body: Convolutional blocks are excellent at building features, but feature maps are not yet the final answer. A CNN still needs a part that reads those features and says, in effect, 'these scores point most strongly to class A.' That part is the classification head.

cards.title: What the classification head does
cards.body: The classification head is the end of the network that converts the last feature maps into class logits. Common heads either flatten the full stack into one long vector or use global pooling to squeeze each channel down to one number, then pass that result through a dense layer to produce one score per class.

cards.title: The judge reads the case summary
cards.body: Think of the earlier CNN blocks as investigators collecting evidence. The head is like the judge at the end. The judge does not revisit every street corner from scratch — the judge reads the summary built by the investigators and then delivers a verdict. The head reads the final features and turns them into class scores.

cards.title: Flatten head vs. global-pooling head
cards.body: Suppose the last feature stack is 4x4x32. A flatten head turns that into 512 numbers, then a dense layer can output 3 logits for three classes. A global-average-pooling head instead turns 4x4x32 into just 32 numbers — one summary per channel — then uses a dense layer to output the same 3 logits. Different route, same goal: class scores.

cards.title: The head does not start vision from zero
cards.body: A beginner might think the dense layer at the end somehow does the 'real' recognition work by itself. Not really. The head depends on the feature extractor before it. It is reading the final learned patterns, not rediscovering the image from scratch.

cards.title: Two common ways into the head
cards.body: Both paths prepare the final feature stack for dense class outputs.

left.text:
4x4x32 -> 512 numbers
Keeps each cell separate
Then dense outputs

right.text:
4x4x32 -> 32 numbers
One average per channel
Then dense outputs

exercises.prompt: Which operation turns a 4x4x32 feature stack into exactly 32 numbers before the dense layer?

options.text: Increasing the spatial size back to 64x64

exercises.explain: Right — global average pooling squeezes each channel down to one summary number.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Flattening and global pooling are both common ways to prepare CNN feature maps for dense class outputs.
exercises.explain: True — both are standard ways to bridge from feature maps to class scores.

exercises.sentence: The dense layer at the end often outputs class ___, which are scores before the final probability step.
exercises.explain: Logits — they are raw class scores, before converting them into final probabilities.

exercises.prompt: Which statement best describes a CNN classification head?

options.text: It converts the last feature maps into class scores, often using flattening or global pooling before a dense layer
It recreates the original image so a human can classify it manually
It exists only to increase spatial size and never to output scores

exercises.explain: Exactly — the classification head is the final feature-to-score translator.

exercises.prompt: A toy CNN ends with a 4x4x16 feature stack and needs to classify 5 classes. Which head description makes the most sense?

options.text: Use global average pooling to get 16 numbers, then a dense layer to output 5 logits
Skip the head entirely and treat the 4x4 grid itself as the final 5 class labels
Convert the feature maps back into a 4x4x3 RGB image, then guess the class by eye
Add pooling forever and never produce class scores

exercises.explain: Exactly. The head's whole job is to turn the last feature stack into class logits. Next we'll put the whole story together by learning how to read a complete CNN architecture diagram from input to output.
